excerpt: The hadith of Jibrīl defining iḥsān as worshipping God as though you see Him, since if you do not see Him, He sees you, is the foundational definition of the highest rank of religious life.
source_location_hint: Kitāb al-Īmān, opening chapter on the ranks of Islām, īmān, and iḥsān

excerpt: The reports on the multiplied reward in Ramaḍān — that a voluntary act in it equals an obligatory act in any other month, and an obligatory act in it equals seventy obligatory acts elsewhere — are transmitted in the Fatimid fiqh tradition.
source_authority: the Imāms of the Ahl al-Bayt
source_location_hint: Kitāb al-Ṣiyām, chapter on the merits of the month of Ramaḍān

excerpt: Do not build what you will not inhabit; do not gather what you will not consume; do not compete for that which you will tomorrow be required to leave.
source_location_hint: section on short sayings and counsels concerning the world (dunyā) and renunciation (zuhd)

excerpt: Sūrat al-Fātiḥa is the Sabʿ al-Mathānī, the Seven Oft-Repeated, and stands as the compressed image of the whole Qurʾān, in which the architecture of the divine summons is set forth in miniature.
source_location_hint: discussion of the Fātiḥa as the locus of the Sabʿ al-Mathānī and the esoteric structure of prayer

excerpt: Imām ʿAlī is identified by the Fatimid tradition as wārith al-nabiyyīn (heir of the prophets), khayr al-waṣiyyīn (best of the legatees), and amīr al-muʾminīn (commander of the faithful), the designated waṣī of the Prophet and the foundation of the walāya.
source_authority: the Imāms of the Ahl al-Bayt
source_location_hint: Kitāb al-Walāya, opening chapters on the designation (naṣṣ) of ʿAlī and his stations
